body_txt: Imagine a story that you're reading/watching going smoothly, without any complications. Something major then occurs, usually pretty shocking for the characters involved, but everything's a little off: the narrator is suddenly acknowledging the characters as if he was involved (maybe even flummoxed), the characters realize things are going wrong, the protagonist realizes that they did something that changed the narrative they're familiar with — this isn't how the story is supposed to go. Things have apparently gone Off the Rails . Also known as "Plot Derailment," going Off the Rails can take many forms, and isn't always malicious or even intentional. A character got tired of their role rebelling against the story they're in . The protagonist is trapped in a world they're familiar with (sometimes reincarnated after their death , reborn as its villainess , or just dropped into the world, taking over someone's life ) and can influence the "canon" narrative. Maybe the protagonist is given another chance to relive their life (via Mental Time Travel or reincarnation ) and can change many things about their previous life. Maybe an actor missed their cue on stage during a play and the production has to be changed on the fly? Or it can simply be a tabletop game player messing up their Game Master 's epic plot with a bad (or too good ) roll. Either way, the audience and (possibly characters In-Universe ) know how the scenario is supposed to play out, but it doesn't, intentionally or not. The origin of this trope is taken from tabletop games like Dungeons & Dragons and the concept of players disrupting a Game Master 's planned storyline. Despite how well plotted their story can be, players can disrupt things because of some Sequence Breaking , an Outside-the-Box Tactic , killing an important NPC that was supposed to survive, saving an NPC that was supposed to die, finding out about an important secret at the wrong moment, suddenly turning evil or turning good , or just refusing to go where the plot demands they should. While its origins stem from tabletop gaming, the use of this trope is much broader, and can be applied to many mediums. In terms of a tabletop game, if the Game Master is inflexible, they can ignore all actions that disrupt their plot , changing the rules so that their plot stays on the rails , or drops a whole ton of rocks on everybody . A more creative GM, on the other hand, will take this player revolt and run with it , spinning a new plot out of the threads of the players' actions, rolling with the punches and adapting the game on the fly or pull something out of thin air . Conversely, the onus isn't always on the GM; whilst sometimes the course of an entire campaign changes for entirely understandable reasons, other times a group full of Loonies or Munchkins are just being disruptive and contrarian for no good reason (i.e. obtusely refusing to engage with anything the GM has prepared For the Lulz ), in which case the GM is entirely justified in taking their ball and leaving. These things can also be applied in a narrative sense too. Sometimes the narrative will give the players a chance to rebel against it, only for fate to allow the story to proceed as planned or the villains' plans go off without a hitch despite some deviations . At its worst, the characters can actively go against the storyteller itself . Occasionally, the plot train can be put back on track with a little help from Schrödinger's Gun and copious amounts of improvising. The winners in this situation are usually all involved. Compare Spanner in the Works , when the plot and plotter being derailed are In-Universe , as opposed to an outside author, and Screw Destiny , where characters decide to go Off the Rails on a more cosmic or godly scale. Contrast Total Party Kill , where the game-ending/story ending disaster comes from incompetence rather than malice or loss of control over the game or narrative. If the nature of things going off the rails is because of the story deviating from The Stations of the Canon via a remake or a premise (fan work or official), those would fall under Adaptation Deviation and its subtropes. Compare Fix Fic , where the plot of the story is to intentionally go off the rails from canon to prevent a character's death or tragedy. If the adaptation makes a change to the premise that drastically makes it an entirely different product compared to the source material, it's an In Name Only adaptation. Not to be confused with Derailing , which is what happens when someone wants to forcibly change a discussion topic or others' plans, or Plot Detour , which can form part of an attempt by an author of a campaign to spin out the story through misdirection or change its direction. Also not to be confused with actual trains coming off actual rails .
